text: The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard focuses on technical specifications for interconnection between utility electric power systems and distributed energy resources.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard focuses on testing requirements for interoperability between utility electric power systems and distributed energy resources.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard provides requirements related to interconnection performance.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard provides requirements related to interoperability performance.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard provides requirements related to operation of distributed energy resources.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard provides requirements related to testing procedures.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard provides requirements related to safety considerations.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard provides requirements related to maintenance considerations.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard provides requirements related to security considerations.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard establishes criteria for interconnection of distributed energy resources with electric power systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard establishes requirements for interconnection of distributed energy resources with electric power systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard defines requirements for associated interfaces between distributed energy resources and electric power systems.
The technical specifications defined in IEEE Std 1547-2018 are universally required for interconnection of distributed energy resources.
The technical specifications defined in IEEE Std 1547-2018 are universally required for interoperability of distributed energy resources.
The requirements defined in IEEE Std 1547-2018 are sufficient for most distributed energy resource installations.
The performance requirements specified in IEEE Std 1547-2018 apply at the time of interconnection.
The performance requirements specified in IEEE Std 1547-2018 continue to apply for the entire duration that the distributed energy resource remains in service.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document provides a uniform standard for interconnection of distributed energy resources with electric power systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document provides a uniform standard for interoperability of distributed energy resources with electric power systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document defines requirements related to interconnection performance.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document defines requirements related to interoperability performance.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document defines requirements related to operation.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document defines requirements related to testing.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document defines requirements related to safety considerations.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document defines requirements related to maintenance considerations.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 document defines requirements related to security considerations.
The criteria defined in IEEE Std 1547-2018 apply to all distributed energy resource technologies interconnected to electric power systems.
The requirements defined in IEEE Std 1547-2018 apply to all distributed energy resource technologies interconnected to electric power systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard applies to distributed energy resources connected at primary distribution voltages.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard applies to distributed energy resources connected at secondary distribution voltages.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard emphasizes installation of distributed energy resources on radial primary distribution systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard emphasizes installation of distributed energy resources on radial secondary distribution systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard also considers installation of distributed energy resources on network distribution systems.
The IEEE Std 1547-2018 standard assumes that distributed energy resources operate at a nominal frequency of 60 Hz.
Figure 1 illustrates the scope of IEEE Std 1547-2018.
The requirements in IEEE Std 1547-2018 may influence the design of the power interface of distributed energy resources.
The requirements in IEEE Std 1547-2018 may influence the capabilities of the power interface of distributed energy resources.
The requirements in IEEE Std 1547-2018 may influence the local distributed energy resource communication interface.
The requirements in IEEE Std 1547-2018 may influence all components of a distributed energy resource that are necessary to meet interconnection requirements.
In Clauses 4 through 11 of IEEE Std 1547-2018, the term "distributed energy resource" refers specifically to components relevant to interconnection and interoperability compliance.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 is not intended for application to energy resources connected to transmission systems.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 is not intended for application to energy resources connected to networked sub-transmission systems.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not define the maximum distributed energy resource capacity that may be interconnected at a single point of common coupling.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not define the maximum distributed energy resource capacity that may be connected to a feeder.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not prescribe self-protection mechanisms for distributed energy resources beyond interconnection requirements.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not prescribe operating requirements for distributed energy resources beyond interconnection compliance.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not address planning of Area Electric Power Systems.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not address design of Area Electric Power Systems.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not address operation of Area Electric Power Systems.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not address maintenance of Area Electric Power Systems.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not apply to automatic transfer schemes where the duration of parallel operation is less than 100 milliseconds.
Emergency distributed energy resources are exempt from certain requirements of IEEE Std 1547-2018.
Standby distributed energy resources are exempt from certain requirements of IEEE Std 1547-2018.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not specify how an Area Electric Power System operator determines parameter settings within allowable ranges.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 does not define communication network specifications for distributed energy resource communication interfaces.
The voltage and frequency trip setting ranges defined in IEEE Std 1547-2018 do not limit the capabilities of other equipment in the Area Electric Power System.
For synchronous generators rated at 10 MVA or greater, the requirements of IEEE Std C50.12 or IEEE Std C50.13 take precedence over IEEE Std 1547-2018 when conflicts occur.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 applies based on the aggregate nameplate rating of all distributed energy resource units within a Local Electric Power System.
Supplemental distributed energy resource devices may be used to achieve compliance with IEEE Std 1547-2018 requirements.
Supplemental distributed energy resource devices are not required to be physically co-located with distributed energy resource units.
Supplemental distributed energy resource devices must be located within the same Local Electric Power System as the distributed energy resource units.

Compliance with IEEE Std 1547-2018 requirements must be achieved regardless of the physical locations of distributed energy resource units and supplemental devices within the Local Electric Power System.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 defines technology-neutral performance categories for distributed energy resources.
The authority governing interconnection requirements determines the applicability of performance categories.
Category A defines minimum reactive power capability requirements for distributed energy resources.
Category A defines minimum voltage regulation performance requirements for distributed energy resources.
Category B includes all requirements defined in Category A.
Category B defines additional capabilities required for systems with high distributed energy resource penetration.
Abnormal operating performance Category I defines minimum requirements for bulk power system stability.
Abnormal operating performance Category II defines requirements to avoid unnecessary tripping during disturbances.
Abnormal operating performance Category III defines requirements for both bulk system stability and distribution-level power quality.
Higher abnormal operating performance categories inherently satisfy ride-through requirements of lower categories.
Higher abnormal operating performance categories may not satisfy trip-setting requirements of lower categories due to different allowable parameter ranges.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 uses generator sign convention for defining electrical quantities.
In generator sign convention, lagging current corresponds to injection of reactive power into the system.
In generator sign convention, leading current corresponds to absorption of reactive power from the system.
The word "shall" indicates a mandatory requirement in IEEE Std 1547-2018.
The word "should" indicates a recommendation in IEEE Std 1547-2018.
The word "may" indicates a permissible action in IEEE Std 1547-2018.
The word "can" indicates possibility or capability in IEEE Std 1547-2018.
Notes in IEEE Std 1547-2018 provide informational content only and do not define mandatory requirements.
The documents listed as normative references in IEEE Std 1547-2018 are indispensable for the application of this standard.
Each referenced document in IEEE Std 1547-2018 shall be understood and used in conjunction with this standard.
Each referenced document in IEEE Std 1547-2018 is cited within the text of the standard.
The relationship between each referenced document and IEEE Std 1547-2018 is explained within the standard.
For dated references in IEEE Std 1547-2018, only the specific cited edition of the referenced document applies.
For undated references in IEEE Std 1547-2018, the latest edition of the referenced document, including amendments and corrigenda, applies.
ANSI C84.1 defines voltage ratings for electric power systems and equipment operating at 60 Hz.
IEEE Std 519 defines recommended practices and requirements for harmonic control in electrical power systems.
IEEE Std 1453 defines recommended practices for analysis of fluctuating installations on power systems.
IEEE Std 1815 defines communication protocols for electric power systems, specifically the Distributed Network Protocol version 3 (DNP3).
IEEE Std 2030.5 defines application protocol standards for smart energy profile communication.
IEEE Std C37.90.1 defines surge withstand capability test requirements for relays and relay systems associated with electric power apparatus.
IEEE Std C37.90.2 defines electromagnetic interference withstand capability requirements for relay systems exposed to radiated interference from transceivers.
The definitions provided in IEEE Std 1547-2018 apply specifically to the interpretation of this document.
Terms not explicitly defined in IEEE Std 1547-2018 should be referenced from the IEEE Standards Dictionary Online.
Defined terms and acronyms are italicized throughout IEEE Std 1547-2018.

An abnormal operating performance category is a grouping of requirements that specifies technical capabilities and parameter settings for a distributed energy resource operating outside the continuous operation region.
Applicable voltage refers to electrical quantities used to determine the performance of a Local Electric Power System or a distributed energy resource at a specified reference point.
Applicable voltage may be defined as phase-to-neutral, phase-to-ground, or phase-to-phase values.
Applicable voltage includes a defined time resolution for measurement.
Applicable voltage is also used as a basis to derive applicable system frequency.
An Area Electric Power System operator is an entity responsible for designing, building, operating, and maintaining an Area Electric Power System.
An Area Electric Power System is an electric power system that serves one or more Local Electric Power Systems.

An authority governing interconnection requirements is an entity responsible for defining, codifying, communicating, administering, and enforcing policies for interconnection of distributed energy resources.
The authority governing interconnection requirements may be a regulatory agency, a public utility commission, a municipality, or a cooperative board.
The authority governing interconnection requirements may delegate responsibilities to an Area Electric Power System operator or a bulk power system operator.
An authority having jurisdiction is an entity responsible for inspection and approval of design and construction of Local Electric Power System electrical installations.
Available active power is the active power that a distributed energy resource can deliver to an Area Electric Power System based on the availability of its primary energy source.
A bulk power system includes electric generation resources, transmission lines, interconnections with neighboring systems, and associated equipment.
To cease to energize means that a distributed energy resource stops exporting active power to an electric power system.
Ceasing to energize may result in momentary cessation or tripping.
Ceasing to energize does not necessarily imply disconnection or isolation.
Ceasing to energize does not exclude continued limited reactive power exchange.
Energy storage systems may continue charging after ceasing to energize until maximum state of charge is reached.
Clearing time is the total time between the start of an abnormal condition and the moment when a distributed energy resource ceases to energize an Area Electric Power System.
Clearing time includes detection time, adjustable delay time, and operating times of switching devices.
Continuous operation is the exchange of current between a distributed energy resource and an electric power system within specified voltage and frequency limits.
Continuous operation requires the distributed energy resource to remain connected to the Area Electric Power System.
A continuous operation region is the operating region corresponding to continuous operation conditions.
A disturbance period is the time interval during which system voltage or frequency is outside the continuous operation region.
Energize refers to the condition where a distributed energy resource exports active power to an electric power system.
Entering service means that a distributed energy resource begins operation while connected to an energized Area Electric Power System.
Interconnection is the result of connecting a distributed energy resource to an Area Electric Power System either directly or through a Local Electric Power System.
Interconnection equipment includes one or more devices used to physically connect a distributed energy resource to an Area Electric Power System.
An interconnection system is the collection of all equipment and functions required to interconnect a distributed energy resource to an Area Electric Power System.
An interface is an electrical or logical connection between two entities that enables energy or data exchange.
Interoperability is the capability of multiple systems or devices to exchange and use information securely and effectively.
Islanding is a condition in which a portion of an Area Electric Power System is energized solely by one or more Local Electric Power Systems.
Islanding occurs when the affected portion of the Area Electric Power System is electrically separated from the rest of the system.
An intentional island is a planned electrical island that includes distributed energy resources and loads.
An intentional island must have the capability to disconnect from and reconnect to the Area Electric Power System.
An intentional Area Electric Power System island includes portions of the Area Electric Power System.
An intentional Local Electric Power System island exists entirely within a Local Electric Power System.
Load refers to devices or processes that consume electrical energy within a Local Electric Power System.
Load excludes energy storage devices that can return energy to the system.
A local distributed energy resource communication interface is a communication interface that supports required information exchange functions for a distributed energy resource.
A Local Electric Power System is an electric power system contained within a single premises or a group of premises.

Mandatory operation is the required continuation of active and reactive current exchange between a distributed energy resource and an Area Electric Power System despite disturbances within defined limits.
A mandatory operation region is the operating region corresponding to mandatory operation conditions.
Medium voltage refers to nominal system voltages greater than or equal to 1 kV and less than or equal to 35 kV.
Momentary cessation is a temporary stop of energy export by a distributed energy resource while remaining connected to the Area Electric Power System.
Momentary cessation allows immediate restoration of output when voltage and frequency return to acceptable ranges.
A momentary cessation operation region defines the conditions under which momentary cessation applies.
Manufacturer stated measurement accuracy is the declared accuracy with which a distributed energy resource measures voltage, current, power, frequency, or time.
Nameplate ratings define nominal voltage, current, maximum active power, apparent power, and reactive power for a distributed energy resource.
A normal operating performance category defines requirements for distributed energy resource operation within the continuous operation region.
Open loop response time is the time required for a system output to reach 90 percent of its final value following a step change input.
Operating mode defines the behavior of a distributed energy resource under normal or abnormal conditions.
A performance operating region is defined by voltage or frequency magnitude and duration limits that specify operational requirements.
Permissive operation is an operating mode in which a distributed energy resource performs ride-through during disturbances.
A permissive operation region defines the conditions under which permissive operation applies.
Permit service is a control setting that determines whether a distributed energy resource is allowed to operate.
Per unit is a normalized quantity expressed as a fraction of a defined base value.
Percent values can be derived from per unit values by multiplying by 100.
The point of common coupling is the location where a Local Electric Power System connects to an Area Electric Power System.
The point of distributed energy resource connection is the location within a Local Electric Power System where a distributed energy resource connects and satisfies standard requirements.
A pre-disturbance period is the time immediately before a disturbance occurs.
A post-disturbance period is the time after system voltage and frequency return to acceptable ranges.
Protective functions are mechanisms designed to maintain safe operation and safe conditions in an electric power system.
Range of allowable settings defines the limits within which control parameters may be adjusted.
Reference point of applicability is the location where performance requirements of IEEE Std 1547-2018 must be satisfied.
A regional reliability coordinator is an entity responsible for maintaining real-time reliability of bulk electric power systems within a defined region.
Restore output refers to returning a distributed energy resource to its pre-disturbance operating state.
Return to service refers to re-entering operation after a trip event.
Ride-through is the ability of a distributed energy resource to remain connected and operational during disturbances within defined limits.
A simulated utility is a test system capable of providing variable voltage and frequency to emulate an Area Electric Power System.
Step response is the time-domain output behavior of a system following a step input.
A supplemental distributed energy resource device is equipment used to help a distributed energy resource meet interconnection requirements.
total demand distortion (TDD):Total rated-current distortion is the root-sum-square of current distortion components expressed as a percentage of rated current.
A trip is a condition that prevents immediate return to service and may involve disconnection.
A type test is a test conducted on representative equipment to verify compliance with design requirements.
An unintentional island is an unplanned islanding condition.
Zero-sequence continuity refers to a circuit topology that maintains continuity in the zero-sequence network between two points.
A transformer with delta or ungrounded wye windings may interrupt zero-sequence continuity.

An unintentional island occurs when a distributed energy resource energizes a portion of an Area Electric Power System through a point of common coupling while that portion is electrically isolated from the rest of the Area Electric Power System.
A distributed energy resource shall detect an unintentional island condition.
A distributed energy resource shall cease to energize the Area Electric Power System after detecting an unintentional island condition.
A distributed energy resource shall trip within 2 seconds after the formation of an unintentional island.
False detection of an unintentional island condition does not justify non-compliance with ride-through requirements specified in Clause 6 of IEEE Std 1547-2018.
The clearing time for ceasing to energize during an unintentional island may be extended from 2 seconds to a maximum of 5 seconds.
Extension of clearing time requires mutual agreement between the Area Electric Power System operator and the distributed energy resource operator.
Requirements for coordination with automatic reclosing in an Area Electric Power System are specified in Clause 6.3 of IEEE Std 1547-2018.

metadata.clause_title: Area Electric Power System with Automatic Reclosing

text: An intentional island may be classified as an intentional Area Electric Power System island or an intentional Local Electric Power System island.
The requirements defined in Clause 8.2 apply to both intentional Area Electric Power System islands and intentional Local Electric Power System islands.
An intentional Area Electric Power System island includes one or more portions of an Area Electric Power System.
An intentional Area Electric Power System island shall be designed in coordination with the Area Electric Power System operator.
An intentional Area Electric Power System island shall be operated in coordination with the Area Electric Power System operator while in islanded mode.
An intentional Local Electric Power System island exists entirely within the boundaries of a Local Electric Power System.

A distributed energy resource that supports intentional Local Electric Power System islands shall comply with all interconnection requirements when connected to a non-islanded Area Electric Power System.
The applicable interconnection requirements for such distributed energy resources are specified in Clauses 4 through 8.1 of IEEE Std 1547-2018.
An intentional island system may transition to islanded operation through scheduled means.
An intentional island system may transition to islanded operation through unscheduled means.
A scheduled intentional island is formed through deliberate actions by a distributed energy resource operator or an Area Electric Power System operator.
A scheduled intentional island may be initiated through manual control actions.
A scheduled intentional island may be initiated through automated dispatch systems such as an Energy Management System.
A scheduled intentional island may be initiated through Automatic Generation Control systems.
Formation of a scheduled intentional island involves transitioning from synchronized parallel operation with the Area Electric Power System to independent islanded operation.
An unscheduled intentional island is formed autonomously without direct operator intervention.
Formation of an unscheduled intentional island is triggered by local detection of abnormal conditions at interconnection points with the Area Electric Power System.
Formation of an unscheduled intentional island involves automatic relay actions.
Automatic relay actions trigger switching operations that isolate the intentional island from the Area Electric Power System.
Isolation of an unscheduled intentional island from the Area Electric Power System shall occur rapidly.
An intentional island may disconnect from the Area Electric Power System when exception conditions defined in Clause 6.4.2.1 are satisfied.

metadata.clause_title: Conditions for Unscheduled Transition to Intentional Island

text: An intentional island may disconnect from the Area Electric Power System when exception conditions defined in Clause 6.5.2.1 are satisfied.

metadata.clause_title: Conditions for Unscheduled Transition to Intentional Island

text: An intentional island may transition to island mode when trip conditions defined in Clause 6 of IEEE Std 1547-2018 are satisfied.

metadata.clause_title: Conditions for Unscheduled Transition to Intentional Island

text: Transition to intentional island mode is allowed in cases where Clause 6 permits or requires tripping.

metadata.clause_title: Conditions for Unscheduled Transition to Intentional Island

text: An intentional island may transition to island mode when conditions for unintentional island detection defined in Clause 8.1 are satisfied.

metadata.clause_title: Conditions for Unscheduled Transition to Intentional Island

text: In such cases, the intentional island may enter intentional island mode instead of ceasing to energize the Area Electric Power System.

metadata.clause_title: Conditions for Unscheduled Transition to Intentional Island

text: The intentional island shall not energize any portion of the Area Electric Power System outside the defined island boundaries during this transition.

metadata.clause_title: Conditions for Unscheduled Transition to Intentional Island

text: An intentional island that is not exempt under Clauses 6.4.2.1 or 6.5.2.1 shall meet the requirements of Clause 7.2.2 when disconnecting from the Area Electric Power System.

metadata.clause_title: Transition of an Intentional Island from the Area Electric Power System

text: The requirement applies when the Area Electric Power System voltage and frequency are within continuous operation ranges defined in Clause 6.

metadata.clause_title: Transition of an Intentional Island from the Area Electric Power System

text: An intentional island may reconnect to the Area Electric Power System after disconnection.

metadata.clause_title: Reconnection of an Intentional Island to the Area Electric Power System

text: Reconnection is permitted when the conditions specified in Clause 4.10 are satisfied at the point of common coupling.

metadata.clause_title: Reconnection of an Intentional Island to the Area Electric Power System

text: Upon reconnection, the intentional island shall meet the requirements specified in Clause 4.10.4 of IEEE Std 1547-2018.

metadata.clause_title: Reconnection of an Intentional Island to the Area Electric Power System

text: Distributed energy resources operating within an intentional Area Electric Power System island may require adjustment of control settings.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Distributed energy resources operating within an intentional Area Electric Power System island may require adjustment of protection settings.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Alternative control and protection settings shall only be enabled when the intentional Area Electric Power System island is electrically isolated from the Area Electric Power System.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Adaptive protection and control mechanisms may be required to implement these alternative settings.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Distributed energy resources operating in an intentional Area Electric Power System island shall comply with reactive power capability requirements defined in Clause 5.2.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Distributed energy resources operating in an intentional Area Electric Power System island shall comply with voltage and reactive power control requirements defined in Clause 5.3.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: The allowable setting range for overvoltage trip function OV2 shall be between 0.008 seconds and 0.16 seconds for all performance categories.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: The allowable setting range for overfrequency trip setting OF1 shall be between 11 seconds and 1000 seconds for all performance categories.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: The allowable setting range for underfrequency trip setting UF1 shall be between 11 seconds and 1000 seconds for all performance categories.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Distributed energy resources shall comply with frequency droop requirements defined in Clause 6.5.2.7.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: For Category III distributed energy resources, the frequency droop parameter kOF shall be adjustable between 0.0055 per unit and 0.05 per unit.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: For Category III distributed energy resources, the lower bound of the allowable range for open-loop response time is not applicable for participating distributed energy resources.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Distributed energy resources shall be capable of operating in Area Electric Power System-connected mode.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Distributed energy resources shall be capable of operating in intentional island mode.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Distributed energy resources shall be capable of transitioning between Area Electric Power System-connected mode and intentional island mode.

metadata.clause_title: Adjustments to Distributed Energy Resource Settings

text: Clauses 4 through 10 of IEEE Std 1547-2018 define interconnection and interoperability requirements for distributed energy resources.
The requirements defined in Clauses 4 through 10 of IEEE Std 1547-2018 shall be tested and verified according to Clause 11.
The requirements in IEEE Std 1547-2018 define conditions necessary for proper operation of distributed energy resources.
The requirements in IEEE Std 1547-2018 define conditions necessary for reliable operation of distributed energy resources.
The requirements include properties of distributed energy resources.
The requirements include properties that must be maintained at operational interfaces.
The requirements include properties that must be maintained throughout the lifetime of an installed distributed energy resource system.
All verification requirements for distributed energy resources shall be satisfied.
All verification requirements for distributed energy resources shall be verified in accordance with IEEE Std 1547.1.
Additional requirements may be imposed depending on the verification method used.
Additional requirements may be imposed to ensure traceability to national standards.
Additional requirements may be imposed to ensure accuracy of recorded data.
Additional requirements may be imposed to account for conditions of the unit under test.
Additional requirements may be imposed to address temporary patches or operational workarounds.
Clause 11 specifies testing and verification methods used to confirm compliance with interconnection and interoperability requirements.
Clause 11 specifies that testing and verification apply at the reference point defined in Clause 4.2.
Clause 11 specifies the stage in the interconnection process at which testing and verification are required.
The testing and verification methods defined in Clause 11 apply to all distributed energy resources.
Results of testing and verification shall be formally documented.
All interconnection and interoperability requirements shall be demonstrated through testing or verification methods.
Acceptable verification methods include type tests, production tests, distributed energy resource evaluation, commissioning tests, periodic tests, or a combination of these methods.
Requirements that are only partially verified through type testing shall be fully verified through distributed energy resource evaluation and commissioning tests.
A type test may be performed on a single device.
A type test may be performed on a combination of devices forming a system.
A type test performed on a system verifies that the devices can operate together as an integrated system.
Type tests shall be conducted on a specific distributed energy resource unit or system as applicable.
Type tests shall be performed on a representative distributed energy resource unit or system.
Type tests may be conducted in a factory environment.
Type tests may be conducted in a testing laboratory.
Type tests may be conducted on equipment installed in the field.
Type test results from one distributed energy resource may be used to represent other distributed energy resources within the same product family.
The product family must share the same hardware design.
The product family must share the same software design.
The applicable power rating range for representation is between 50 percent and 200 percent of the tested unit rating.
Replacement of distributed energy resource equipment with compliant substitute components in the field does not invalidate previous type test results.
Field demonstration of performance after equipment replacement shall be agreed upon by the Area Electric Power System operator and the distributed energy resource operator.
Production tests shall be conducted on every distributed energy resource unit prior to customer delivery.
Production tests shall verify compliance with applicable standards.
Production tests shall confirm the operability of each distributed energy resource unit.
Production tests shall confirm the operability of interconnection equipment.
Production test reports shall include performance category capabilities.
Production test reports shall include final function settings.
Production test reports shall include final operating mode settings.
Manufacturers with certified production facilities may use approved quality control processes to satisfy production test requirements.
Certification must be provided by a nationally recognized testing laboratory.
Replacement of distributed energy resource equipment with compliant components in the field does not invalidate previous production test results.
Field performance verification after replacement shall be agreed upon by the Area Electric Power System operator and the distributed energy resource operator.
Distributed energy resource evaluation includes a design evaluation and an as-built installation evaluation.
Distributed energy resource evaluation verifies that a system composed of partially compliant components meets interconnection requirements.
Distributed energy resource evaluation applies when supplemental distributed energy resource devices are used.
Design evaluation is performed during the interconnection review process.
Design evaluation verifies that the system design meets interconnection and interoperability requirements.
Design evaluation is typically performed before equipment delivery and installation.
As-built installation evaluation is conducted at the time of commissioning.
As-built installation evaluation verifies that the installed system meets interconnection requirements.
As-built installation evaluation does not require active testing procedures.
A basic distributed energy resource evaluation verifies correct installation and component selection.
A detailed distributed energy resource evaluation includes engineering validation of system components.
A detailed distributed energy resource evaluation may include modeling and simulation of system performance.
Commissioning tests verify that installed systems meet interconnection and interoperability requirements.
Commissioning tests may be performed on a single device or a combination of devices.
Commissioning tests shall follow documented test procedures.
Test procedures shall be provided by equipment manufacturers or system designers.
Test procedures shall be approved by the equipment owner and the Area Electric Power System operator.
Commissioning tests shall include visual inspections.
Commissioning tests may include operability tests.
Commissioning tests may include functional performance tests.
A basic functional commissioning test includes visual inspection.
A basic functional commissioning test includes operability testing of isolation devices.
A detailed functional commissioning test includes all elements of a basic test.
A detailed functional commissioning test includes verification of interoperability among system components.
A detailed functional commissioning test verifies coordinated operation of multiple devices within a system.
Periodic tests are conducted at scheduled intervals or based on defined criteria.
Periodic tests verify continued compliance of interconnected systems.
Periodic test records shall be maintained for inspection.
Requirements define system capability, design, and performance expectations.
Testing and verification confirm that requirements have been satisfied.
Testing requirements are defined using test requirement matrices.
Test requirement matrices provide traceability between requirements and verification methods.
Test requirement matrices ensure consistency across interconnection testing stages.
Minimum testing requirements are defined in the test requirement matrices.
Additional testing requirements may be imposed by the Area Electric Power System operator.
Separate test requirement matrices exist for verification at the point of common coupling and the point of distributed energy resource connection.
Performance requirements may apply at either the point of common coupling or the point of distributed energy resource connection.
The applicable location depends on distributed energy resource rating and load conditions within the Local Electric Power System.
Equipment type testing may be sufficient for verifying compliance at the point of distributed energy resource connection.
Additional verification methods are required for compliance at the point of common coupling.
Additional verification methods include distributed energy resource evaluation and commissioning tests.
Testing and verification procedures may be used to demonstrate compliance at the point of common coupling.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: Additional requirements may be imposed by the Area Electric Power System operator.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: Distributed energy resource systems shall be classified as fully compliant or partially compliant.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: Required tests shall be conducted based on the classification.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: System design verification shall confirm compliance with fault response requirements.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: Type tests shall verify open-phase condition performance requirements.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: Certification records shall include transformer vector group configurations.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: Additional verification may be performed through distributed energy resource evaluation or commissioning tests.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of Common Coupling

text: Testing and verification procedures may be used to demonstrate compliance at the point of distributed energy resource connection.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of DER Connection

text: Test requirement matrices define verification requirements for this location.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of DER Connection

text: Distributed energy resource units shall be classified as fully compliant or partially compliant.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of DER Connection

text: Required verification methods shall be applied according to classification.

metadata.clause_title: Verification at the Point of DER Connection

text: Fault current characterization defines testing and documentation requirements for distributed energy resource fault contributions.
Requirements apply to electronically coupled distributed energy resources with aggregate ratings of 500 kVA or greater.
Requirements apply to three-phase systems composed of multiple single-phase systems if aggregate rating exceeds 500 kVA.
The purpose of testing is to determine controller response under fault conditions.
These requirements exclude synchronous and induction generators without active rotor current control.
Distributed energy resource operators shall provide voltage and current waveform data for all three phases.
Measurement data shall be obtained during type testing.
Sequence impedance characteristics of the external test source shall be documented.
Distributed energy resources shall be tested for maximum short-circuit current capability.
Data requirements include nameplate apparent power rating.
Data requirements include synchronous impedance.
Data requirements include negative sequence impedance.
Data requirements include zero sequence impedance.
Data requirements include transient impedance.
Data requirements include subtransient impedance.
These requirements exclude induction generators with active rotor current control.
IEEE Std 1547-2018 includes a (informative) bibliography as Annex A that lists citations referred to in this standard for informative purposes.
DER Equivalent to “distributed resources (DR)” as defined and used in IEEE Std 1547-2003.

The technical specifications for, and testing of, the interconnection and interoperability between utility electric power systems (EPSs) and distributed energy resources (DERs) are the focus of this standard.
This standard does not define the maximum DER capacity for a particular installation that may be interconnected to a single point of common coupling (PCC) or connected to a given feeder.

type test: A test of one or more devices manufactured to a certain design to demonstrate, or provide information that can be used to verify, that the design meets the requirements specified in this standard.

The design evaluation (desk study) is an evaluation during the interconnection review process to verify that the composite of the individual partially compliant DERs forming a system as designed meets the interconnection and interoperability requirements of this standard. This evaluation is usually done off-site before equipment is delivered and installed.
Distributed generation (DG) consists of electric generation facilities connected to an Area EPS through a PCC

The DR shall be operated in parallel with a predominantly inductive voltage source with a short circuit current capacity ISC of not less than 20 times the DR rated output current at fundamental frequency.

In this standard only current distortion limits are defined. The methodology for setting current distortion limits and for distortion measurement are adopted from IEEE Std 519. There are a few intentional, and notable, differences. The first is a change from total harmonic distortion (THD) limits, used in 2003, to total rated current distortion (TRD).
The DR shall be operated at an output test load current, IL, of 33%, 66%, and at a level as close to 100% of rated output current as practical.

A basic DER evaluation shall be limited to verify that the DER has been designed and installed with the proper components and connections. A detailed DER evaluation shall include an engineering verification of the chosen components and may require modeling and simulation of the composite of the individual partially compliant DERs forming a system.
The applicable tests of section 5.1 shall be repeated when functional software or firmware changes have been made on the interconnection system.

The cease to energize functionality test shall check the cease to energize functionality by operating a load interrupting device and verifying the equipment ceases to energize its output terminals and does not restart/reconnect for the required time delay.
The cease to energize functionality test shall be performed on each phase individually.

All interconnection-related protective functions and associated batteries shall be periodically tested at intervals specified by the manufacturer, system integrator, or the authority who has jurisdiction over the DR interconnection.
total demand distortion (TDD): The total root-sum-square harmonic current distortion, in percent of the maximum demand load current (15 or 30 minute demand).

The DR shall be operated in parallel with a predominantly inductive voltage source with a short circuit cur rent capacity ISC of not less than 20 times the DR rated output current at fundamental frequency. The voltage and frequency output of the voltage source shall correspond to the rated voltage and frequency of the DR. The unloaded voltage waveform produced by the Area EPS or simulated utility voltage source shall have a total harmonic distortion (THD) less than 2.5%.
Individual harmonic order h < 11
Isolation device A system design verification shall be made to ensure that the requirements have been met.
Monitoring provisions A system design verification shall be made to ensure that the provisions for monitoring are in accordance with 1547-2003-4.1.6.

electric power system, area (Area EPS): An EPS that serves Local EPSs. NOTE—Typically, an Area EPS has primary access to public rights-of-way, priority crossing of property boundaries, etc., and is subject to regulatory oversight.
Response to abnormal voltage and frequency

Other unintentional islanding functionality tests If tests in 2003-5.4.1.1 and 2003-5.4.1.2 are not applicable to the interconnection system, the interconnection system shall be tested in accordance with procedures provided by the manufacturer or system integrator.
For induction interconnection, the induction machine shall be tested to determine the maximum start-up in-rush current.
